review_text: Fun and strong characters, incredible writing, maybe the best ever blocking, and some of the best suspense filmmaking put on film.
Yes, it's wonderfully filmed for 1954, but let's be honest: the only real reason to watch this is Grace Kelly!
As much of an A-grade, firing-on-all-cylinders, deceptively linear and simplistic exercise on marriage and murder as you'll get.

This is one of my personal favorite films, and a testament to the art of cinema. It stars James Stewart as a wheelchair-bound photographer, who passes the time by window-watching. Building a film on such a simple concept is realistically something that would never be done today, and I find it inspirational. There is so much to see in the mundane. Every inch of screen in every frame is perfectly crafted. Elaborate sets, hand-painted backgrounds, and a creativity you can't help but fall in love with. The set is as much of a character as a human could be. Rear Window is filled with twists and turns, perfect pacing, witty dialogue, and excellent performances from the entire cast. There is a reason why it is so heavily praised. It is the perfect study of humanity, through the lens of a camera.
I've seen the movie 5 times. And it truly gets better Every time. Thelma Ritter steals every scene she's in and of course Jimmy Stewart is excellent.
Well crafted, well acted and deceptively intricate with the neighbours vignettes
Hard to believe that most of this film takes place inside an apartment and that somehow you are entertained all throughout.
Movie perfection. A classic.
Probably my favorite Hitchcock film. Stewart is a really good actor.
A witty mirror held up to show the voyeur in all of us. Grace Kelly was never more alluring nor James Stewart's world-weariness better used.

Compared to the Foreign Correspondent that I watched about ten days ago and found quite mediocre, here Hitchcock has reached creative maturity. The film is intentionally ambiguous. Is the guy James Stewat is observing really sinister or him being restricted to his apartment with a broken leg has put his imagination on overdrive? And there are other subplots to consider. Is the lonely older woman going to kill herself? And then we have the magnificent Grace Kelly who looks absolutely superb and easy to understand how she ended up as princess in real life. There are organic elements of comedy and nicely restored color cinematography.

A classic mystery thriller by the master of suspense, Alfred Hitchcock. Stars James Stewart, Grace Kelly, Wendall Corey,Thelma Ritter and Raymond Burr in an effective role as a suspected murderer. Stewart plays a recuperating news photographer confined to a wheelchair who has a bird's eye view of his rear window where the lives of tenants of neighboring apartments play out before him, including a possible murder.
Absolutely amazing movie.

A classic by Alfred Hitchcock. It’s very well written, creating an engaging dynamic between the two leads that heightens as the mysterious events unfold around them. The film is creatively set entirely inside an apartment, from which our protagonist observes his surroundings; a clever reflection of his inner conflict that sets up an intriguing plot. The performances are strong; even for their time, they feel precise and convincing. It’s almost needless to say, but the direction is impeccable. The camerawork and editing maintain rhythm and tension, keeping us interested (even excited) despite the film’s single location and largely immobile protagonist. During the third act, the film may ask viewers to suspend disbelief for a few moments, but it earns that right through its masterful buildup, though some audiences might not fully buy into it. Others may also find parts of its dialogue or style dated, but as a Hitchcock classic, and a landmark in cinema history, it’s definitely a must-see.
Perhaps the most elegant and satisfying of Alfred Hitchcock’s thrillers.
This is when movie stars were movie stars. Story line is brilliant and Grace Kelly is a princess. Watch out for Perry M.

The suspense and buildup is superb. All the things leading up to the end really keeps the audience engaged. Assisting in the execution is the casting. Jimmy Stewart and Grace Kelly carried the film. Great movie overall.

A very well-made movie, and I can easily see why it’s considered a classic. Easily the best thing here is the cinematography, with the entire movie essentially being composed of like four shots, in a single location. Every character feels like a realistic, compelling person, and the plot itself, while simplistic and drawn-out, was captivating. The only main issue for me was the music. At first it feels like a tonal choice, creating dissonance with the tense story, but it plays throughout the entire film. Having tense sequences, like a main character breaking into the antagonist’s apartment, with cheerful, romantic jazz music in the background isn’t incongruent affect, it’s inconsistency. And the fact that the best scene in the movie was easily the buildup to the final fight (though the “fight” using flash bulbs and reused footage was awful), which didn’t use any music and relied completely on tension, speaks volumes. In conclusion, while I didn’t love this movie (I honestly prefer the Simpsons parody episode), I still massively respect it.
My favorite movie of all time, Might be subjective but the acting music I love everything about this movie.
Boring, slow and don't know why people call it a thriller. Buddy just sits and watches out a window all day.

This classic is paradoxical in that I find it both suspenseful and relaxing at once. There's obviously plenty of starpower here and your first veiwing should be a terrific experience. But after that it's the kind of film you can use as a background to help you fall asleep. Sleep is important; it's good to have a few favorite movies that do the trick!

This one was primarily a slow burn for me with the exception of one scene in which Grace Kelly’s character does some risky sleuthing and the last 10-minutes of the film which was suspenseful in proper Hitchcock style. The neighbors’ apartments were the perfect backdrop for this movie.
The shots, angles, lighting, music, performance… everything is perfect.

Rear Window is a classic suspense movie from the golden age of Hollywood. I thought it was pretty boring. I think if you watch a lot of whodunit movies, and thrillers, many of these tropes will seem very trite. However, I imagine for midcentury audiences, it was fascinating and engaging. I thought the acting was fine, and the dialogue great, but the story just took forever to get anywhere.

Would-be thriller in which the protagonist is never in danger -- and it is uncertain whether anything is at stake -- until the final five minutes of the film. Hitchcock violates his own theory of suspense here, arousing only mild curiosity instead. The film's inciting incident also takes place just minutes before its conclusion, forcing the audience to spend most of the runtime wondering when the story will begin in earnest. Memorable only for its voyeuristic themes and visual idiosyncrasies.
Love that the set production was Al ost like a theater set up with minimal set changes .. and yet the plot was enticingly interesting. A great who done it. Totally recommend.
Hitchcock's defining (or one of his) masterpiece(s). The most seamless combination of romance, suspense, engaging drama, humour and charm. 'Rear Window' is a world beyond timeless.

I've been slowly but steadily watching Hitchcock films and this is the first I've found some personal enthusiasm for. This like all the others brings a fascinating esthetic, but it also brings an engaging, immersive story and an excellent cast. The set itself is quite the character in this film. I'm very glad I found a Hitchcock film to demonstrate and justify his celebrated status. Rear Window is high quality.

Idk why people liked it so much. Jimmy Stewart is a great actor but the character he plays here is imo very nosy and dumb. Grace Kelly doesn't act but just looks pretty. The plot is contrived and ridiculous and I felt dirty for sympathizing with Stewart's character. Kelly's character was beyond cringe near the end. Did not like the movie. 2.5 stars for the little dog.

Hitchcock apresenta um êxito indiscutível em construir um ponto de vista ao mesmo tempo subjetivo e palpável. Assim ele envolve a audiência ao redor da curiosidade de Jeff no mesmo ritmo que envolve os demais personagens. Fica uma sensação de recompensa aquém da excitação acumulada ao longo do filme, mas a jornada compensa muito.

1954's 'Rear Window' is the definition of what a class movie is, and is one of Alfred Hitchcock's best films. SYNOPSIS: 'A bored photographer recovering from a broken leg passes the time by watching his neighbors and begins to suspect one of them of murder.' 'Rear Window' works just as well today as it did when Alfred Hitchcock made it in 1954. The film is a cultural reference point of it's time and it's production values are second to none. The cast is as perfect as everything else about this superior thriller. James Stewart was just an incredible actor. 'Rear Window' spares nothing in its build up and its climax, which it achieves with the masterly touches for which its guide and creator is distinguished. The perfect movie? Very much so. 10/10
Though not the most creatively written, Rear Window is one of Hitchcock's best and his most cleverly made.
